summary: Cantrill celebrates the OpenSolaris launch day by curating the overwhelming flood of technical blog posts from Sun engineers. He highlights key entries across fault management, virtual memory, linking/loading, and scheduling subsystems, providing guided tours through the intricacies of Solaris kernel internals. The post serves as both a celebration of the grass-roots open source effort and a reading guide to help the community navigate over 200,000 words of technical content published in a single day.
key_points: OpenSolaris launched with hundreds of blog entries totaling over 200,000 words (800+ pages) in one day
The post curates technical deep-dives across fault management, virtual memory, linking/loading, and scheduling
Engineers finally get to publicly discuss the system they've poured their lives into
The breadth of blogging demonstrates this is a genuine grass-roots effort, not corporate mandate
money_quotes: The breadth and depth of our blogging will hopefully put to rest any notion that open sourcing Solaris isn't a grass-roots effort: if nothing else, it should be clear that we in the trenches are very excited to finally be able to talk about the system that we have poured so much of our lives into – and to welcome new would-be contributors into the fold.
One of the most polished subsystems in Solaris is the linker and loader – the craftsmanship of the engineers that have built it has been an ongoing inspiration for many of us in Solaris development.
If nothing else, you'll see from David's work the research element of operating system development: we often aren't assured of success when we endeavor to improve the system.
You'll see from Eric's odyssey that scheduling problems can require a lot of brain-bending (and patience) to debug!
key_insight: The massive, spontaneous outpouring of deeply technical blog posts from Solaris engineers on OpenSolaris launch day demonstrates that open sourcing was a genuine grass-roots effort driven by engineers eager to share the systems they'd spent their careers building.
